content.content:
1	# Adding New Intrinsics
2	
3	So you want to teach cuda-oxide a new GPU trick. Maybe NVIDIA just shipped a new
4	instruction, or you need an existing PTX operation that nobody has wired up yet.
5	Good news: the process is mechanical. Five crates, five steps, and roughly thirty
6	minutes once you have done it before.
7	
8	This chapter walks through the full pipeline using two real examples -- one
9	trivially simple, one with a few twists -- so you can see exactly what happens at
10	each stage.
11	
12	---
13	
14	## The Five-Stage Pipeline
15	
16	Every GPU intrinsic follows the same path through the compiler:
17	
18	```text
19	cuda-device          User writes:  thread::threadIdx_x()
20	    │
21	    ▼
22	mir-importer       Compiler sees the call, emits a `dialect-nvvm` op
23	    │
24	    ▼
25	dialect-nvvm       The op lives here as a verified IR node
26	    │
27	    ▼
28	mir-lower          Converts the `dialect-nvvm` op into an LLVM dialect op
29	    │
30	    ▼
31	llvm-export        Exports textual LLVM IR  →  llc turns it into PTX
32	```
33	
34	At the `mir-lower` stage you pick one of two strategies:
35	
36	| Strategy            | When to use                                                                  | Examples                               |
37	| :------------------ | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------------------------- |
38	| LLVM intrinsic call | LLVM already has a built-in for it                                           | `threadIdx_x`, warp shuffles, barriers |
39	| Inline PTX assembly | No LLVM intrinsic exists, or you need exact control over the PTX instruction | `trap`, `wgmma`, `tcgen05`, `mbarrier` |
40	
41	Both strategies are demonstrated below.
42	
43	---
44	
45	## Example 1: `threadIdx_x` (the Simple Case)
46	
47	`threadIdx_x()` is the "Hello, World" of GPU intrinsics: zero arguments, one
48	`u32` result, maps directly to a single LLVM NVVM intrinsic. If you can follow
49	this example, you can add any simple intrinsic.
50	
51	### Stage 1 -- Declare in `cuda-device`
52	
53	**File:** `crates/cuda-device/src/thread.rs`
54	
55	```rust
56	#[inline(never)]
57	pub fn threadIdx_x() -> u32 {
58	    unreachable!("threadIdx_x called outside CUDA kernel context")
59	}
60	```
61	
62	Two rules that might look odd until you understand the trick:
63	
64	- **`#[inline(never)]`** keeps the function visible as a distinct call in MIR.
65	  If rustc inlined it, the compiler would see `unreachable!()` instead of a
66	  call it can intercept. That would be... less than helpful.
67	
68	- **The body is `unreachable!()`** because this function never actually runs.
69	  The compiler replaces the entire call with a `dialect-nvvm` operation
70	  before any GPU code executes. Think of the function as a placeholder -- a
71	  "dear compiler, please insert a GPU instruction here" note.
72	
73	```{tip}
74	Document what LLVM IR or PTX the intrinsic maps to in a comment above the
75	function. Future you (or future contributors) will thank present you.
76	```
77	
78	### Stage 2 -- Define the `dialect-nvvm` Op
79	
80	**File:** `crates/dialect-nvvm/src/ops/thread.rs`
81	
82	```rust
83	#[pliron_op(name = "nvvm.read_ptx_sreg_tid_x", dialect = "nvvm", format)]
84	pub struct ReadPtxSregTidXOp;
85	
86	impl Verify for ReadPtxSregTidXOp {
87	    fn verify(&self, ctx: &Context) -> Result<(), Error> {
88	        let op = &*self.get_operation().deref(ctx);
89	        if op.get_num_operands() != 0 {
90	            return verify_err!(op.loc(), "expected 0 operands");
91	        }
92	        if op.get_num_results() != 1 {
93	            return verify_err!(op.loc(), "expected 1 result");
94	        }
95	        Ok(())
96	    }
97	}
98	```
99	
100	Then register it so pliron knows the op exists:
101	
102	```rust
103	pub(super) fn register(ctx: &mut Context) {
104	    ReadPtxSregTidXOp::register(ctx, ReadPtxSregTidXOp::parser_fn);
105	}
106	```
107	
108	The `Verify` trait catches structural bugs early. If something accidentally
109	creates this op with two operands, verification fails with a clear message
110	instead of producing garbage PTX three stages later.
111	
112	### Stage 3 -- Recognize in `mir-importer`
113	
114	**File:** `crates/mir-importer/src/translator/terminator/mod.rs`
115	
116	When the translator processes MIR, every function call passes through
117	`try_dispatch_intrinsic()`. The callee's fully qualified domain name (FQDN)
118	comes from `CrateDef::name()` via `extract_func_info()`, producing paths like
119	`cuda_device::thread::threadIdx_x`. The match checks this FQDN:
120	
121	```rust
122	match name {
123	    "cuda_device::threadIdx_x" | "cuda_device::thread::threadIdx_x" => {
124	        Ok(Some(helpers::emit_nvvm_intrinsic(
125	            ctx,
126	            ReadPtxSregTidXOp::get_concrete_op_info(),
127	            destination, target, block_ptr, prev_op,
128	            value_map, block_map, loc,
129	        )?))
130	    }
131	    // ... hundreds of other intrinsics ...
132	}
133	```
134	
135	We match both the re-exported name (`cuda_device::threadIdx_x`) and the full
136	module path (`cuda_device::thread::threadIdx_x`) so both work regardless of how
137	the user imports the function. The FQDN is used as-is for matching -- no `::` to
138	`__` conversion happens before the intrinsic check.
139	
140	The `emit_nvvm_intrinsic()` helper is a generic function that works for *any*
141	zero-argument, single-result NVVM intrinsic. It creates the operation, stores
142	the result in the value map, and emits a branch to the next basic block. For
143	simple intrinsics, you never need to write a custom emitter.
144	
145	### Stage 4 -- Lower to the LLVM dialect
146	
147	**File:** `crates/mir-lower/src/convert/intrinsics/basic.rs`
148	
149	The op implements the `MirToLlvmConversion` op interface. In
150	`convert/interface_impls.rs`, the impl dispatches to a converter function:
151	
152	```rust
153	impl MirToLlvmConversion for ReadPtxSregTidXOp {
154	    fn rewrite(ctx, rewriter, op, operands_info) -> Result<()> {
155	        basic::convert_read_tid_x(ctx, rewriter, op, operands_info)
156	    }
157	}
158	```
159	
160	Then `basic::convert_read_tid_x` emits the LLVM intrinsic call:
161	
162	```rust
163	pub fn convert_read_tid_x(
164	    ctx: &mut Context,
165	    rewriter: &mut DialectConversionRewriter,
166	    op: Ptr<Operation>,
167	    _operands_info: &OperandsInfo,
168	) -> Result<()> {
169	    let intrinsic_name = "llvm_nvvm_read_ptx_sreg_tid_x";
170	    let i32_ty = IntegerType::get(ctx, 32, Signedness::Signless);
171	    let func_ty = FuncType::get(ctx, i32_ty.into(), vec![], false);
172	
173	    let call_op = call_intrinsic(ctx, rewriter, intrinsic_name, func_ty, vec![])?;
174	    rewriter.replace_operation_with_values(ctx, op, vec![result]);
175	    Ok(())
176	}
177	```
178	
179	```{note}
180	LLVM intrinsic names use dots (`llvm.nvvm.read.ptx.sreg.tid.x`), but pliron
181	identifiers cannot contain dots. Internally we use underscores
182	(`llvm_nvvm_read_ptx_sreg_tid_x`). The export stage converts them back.
183	```
184	
185	### Stage 5 -- Export (Nothing to Change)
186	
187	**File:** `crates/llvm-export/src/export/` (the export module)
188	
189	The `CallOp` exporter already handles the underscore-to-dot conversion:
190	
191	```rust
192	let fixed_name = if name.starts_with("llvm_nvvm") {
193	    name.replace('_', ".")
194	} else {
195	    strip_device_prefix(&name)
196	};
197	```
198	
199	Since `threadIdx_x` is *not* convergent (it is a per-thread register read,
200	not a collective operation), there is nothing to add to
201	`is_convergent_intrinsic()`.
202	
203	**Final output:**
204	
205	```llvm
206	declare i32 @llvm.nvvm.read.ptx.sreg.tid.x()
207	%v5 = call i32 @llvm.nvvm.read.ptx.sreg.tid.x()
208	```
209	
210	After `llc`:
211	
212	```text
213	mov.u32  %r1, %tid.x;
214	```
215	
216	Five stages, one PTX instruction. Not bad.
217	
218	---
219	
220	## Example 2: `shuffle_xor` (the Complex Case)
221	
222	Warp shuffles are more interesting. The user passes two arguments, but the
223	underlying LLVM intrinsic expects *four*. The compiler quietly fills in the
224	extras. The operation is also **convergent**, meaning LLVM must not move,
225	duplicate, or speculate it across control flow.
226	
227	### Stage 1 -- Declare in `cuda-device`
228	
229	**File:** `crates/cuda-device/src/warp.rs`
230	
231	```rust
232	#[inline(never)]
233	pub fn shuffle_xor(var: u32, lane_mask: u32) -> u32 {
234	    let _ = (var, lane_mask);
235	    unreachable!("shuffle_xor called outside CUDA kernel context")
236	}
237	```
238	
239	Same pattern as before. The `let _ = (var, lane_mask);` suppresses
240	unused-variable warnings -- a small courtesy that costs nothing.
241	
242	### Stage 2 -- Define the `dialect-nvvm` Op
243	
244	**File:** `crates/dialect-nvvm/src/ops/warp.rs`
245	
246	```rust
247	#[pliron_op(name = "nvvm.shfl_sync_bfly_i32", dialect = "nvvm", format)]
248	pub struct ShflSyncBflyI32Op;
249	
250	impl Verify for ShflSyncBflyI32Op {
251	    fn verify(&self, ctx: &Context) -> Result<(), Error> {
252	        let op = &*self.get_operation().deref(ctx);
253	        if op.get_num_operands() != 2 {
254	            return verify_err!(op.loc(), "expected 2 operands");
255	        }
256	        if op.get_num_results() != 1 {
257	            return verify_err!(op.loc(), "expected 1 result");
258	        }
259	        Ok(())
260	    }
261	}
262	```
263	
264	Two operands this time (value and lane mask), one result.
265	
266	### Stage 3 -- Recognize in `mir-importer`
267	
268	**File:** `crates/mir-importer/src/translator/terminator/mod.rs`
269	
270	Unlike `threadIdx_x`, this calls a **specialized emitter** because it needs to
271	handle the user's two arguments:
272	
273	```rust
274	"cuda_device::warp::shuffle_xor" => Ok(Some(
275	    intrinsics::warp::emit_warp_shuffle_i32(
276	        ctx, body,
277	        ShflSyncBflyI32Op::get_concrete_op_info(),
278	        args, destination, target, ...
279	    )?
280	)),
281	```
282	
283	**File:** `crates/mir-importer/src/translator/terminator/intrinsics/warp.rs`
284	
285	```rust
286	pub fn emit_warp_shuffle_i32(ctx, body, shuffle_opid, args, ...) {
287	    if args.len() != 2 { return error; }
288	
289	    let (val, _) = translate_operand(ctx, body, &args[0], ...);
290	    let (lane_or_mask, _) = translate_operand(ctx, body, &args[1], ...);
291	
292	    let shuffle_op = Operation::new(ctx, shuffle_opid,
293	        vec![u32_type.to_ptr()],          // results: [u32]
294	        vec![val, lane_or_mask],          // operands: [value, mask]
295	        vec![], 0,
296	    );
297	
298	}
299	```
300	
301	The emitter translates the user's MIR operands into pliron values --
302	typically the results of `mir.load`s from each operand's alloca slot, or
303	`mir.constant`s for literal arguments -- and wires them into the NVVM
304	operation. (These are not SSA values yet; `pliron::opts::mem2reg` will
305	collapse the load/store chains once translation is complete.) For intrinsics
306	with different argument patterns, you write a similar custom emitter.
307	
308	### Stage 4 -- Lower to the LLVM dialect
309	
310	**File:** `crates/mir-lower/src/convert/intrinsics/warp.rs`
311	
312	Here is where it gets interesting. The LLVM intrinsic for butterfly shuffle
313	takes **four** arguments, not two:
314	
315	```text
316	User's API:     shuffle_xor(value, lane_mask)            →  2 args
317	LLVM intrinsic: shfl.sync.bfly.i32(mask, value, lane_mask, clamp) →  4 args
318	                                    ^^^^                   ^^^^^
319	                                    always -1              always 31
320	```
321	
322	The converter adds the compiler-supplied constants:
323	
324	```rust
325	fn convert_shuffle_i32(op, ctx, intrinsic_name, clamp: i32) {
326	    let operands = get_operands(op, ctx)?;
327	    let (val, lane_or_delta) = (operands[0], operands[1]);
328	
329	    let mask_val  = create_i32_const(ctx, -1);   // all 32 lanes participate
330	    let clamp_val = create_i32_const(ctx, clamp); // full warp width
331	
332	    let func_ty = FuncType::get(ctx.ctx, i32_ty.into(),
333	        vec![i32_ty, i32_ty, i32_ty, i32_ty], false);
334	
335	    let call_op = call_intrinsic(ctx, intrinsic_name, func_ty,
336	        vec![mask_val, val, lane_or_delta, clamp_val]);
337	
338	    map_result(op, call_op, ctx);
339	}
340	```
341	
342	The mask value of `-1` (all bits set) means all 32 lanes in the warp
343	participate. The clamp value of `31` means the shuffle wraps at the full warp
344	width. These are the right defaults for almost every use case, and exposing
345	them to the user would just be noise.
346	
347	### Stage 5 -- Export (Add Convergent)
348	
349	Warp shuffles are **convergent** -- LLVM must not reorder them relative to
350	control flow. The export step checks `is_convergent_intrinsic()`:
351	
352	```rust
353	fn is_convergent_intrinsic(name: &str) -> bool {
354	    name == "llvm.nvvm.barrier0"
355	        || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.shfl")      // shuffles
356	        || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.vote")       // votes
357	        || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.mbarrier")   // async barriers
358	        || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.cp.async.bulk")
359	        // ...
360	}
361	```
362	
363	If your new intrinsic is convergent, add it here. If you forget, LLVM might
364	hoist it out of an `if` block, and your warp-level code will produce wrong
365	results or deadlock. Not great.
366	
367	**Final output:**
368	
369	```llvm
370	declare i32 @llvm.nvvm.shfl.sync.bfly.i32(i32, i32, i32, i32)
371	
372	%v8 = call i32 @llvm.nvvm.shfl.sync.bfly.i32(
373	    i32 -1, i32 %v3, i32 %v4, i32 31) #0
374	
375	attributes #0 = { convergent }
376	```
377	
378	After `llc`:
379	
380	```text
381	shfl.sync.bfly.b32  %r3, %r1, %r2, 31;
382	```
383	
384	---
385	
386	## The Inline PTX Path
387	
388	Some operations do not have LLVM intrinsics. For those, we emit inline PTX
389	assembly directly. The helper `inline_asm_convergent()` handles the boilerplate:
390	
391	```rust
392	// wgmma fence: no inputs, no outputs, just a side effect
393	inline_asm_convergent(
394	    ctx, void_ty.into(), vec![],
395	    "wgmma.fence.sync.aligned;", ""
396	);
397	
398	// mbarrier arrive: one input (pointer), one output (token)
399	inline_asm_convergent(
400	    ctx, i64_ty.into(), vec![ptr_val],
401	    "mbarrier.arrive.shared.b64 $0, [$1];", "=l,r"
402	);
403	```
404	
405	The constraint string follows LLVM inline assembly syntax:
406	
407	| Constraint | Meaning                                 |
408	| :--------- | :-------------------------------------- |
409	| `=l`       | Output: 64-bit register                 |
410	| `=r`       | Output: 32-bit register                 |
411	| `r`        | Input: 32-bit register                  |
412	| `l`        | Input: 64-bit register                  |
413	| (empty)    | No inputs or outputs (side-effect only) |
414	
415	The `sideeffect convergent` markers on the inline assembly tell LLVM to leave
416	it alone -- do not move it, do not delete it, do not duplicate it.
417	
418	---
419	
420	## End-to-End: The Full Journey
421	
422	Here is every representation `threadIdx_x` passes through, top to bottom:
423	
424	```text
425	Rust:         thread::threadIdx_x()
426	MIR:          _3 = threadIdx_x() -> bb1
427	Pliron MIR:   %v = nvvm.read_ptx_sreg_tid_x : i32
428	Pliron LLVM:  %v = call i32 @llvm_nvvm_read_ptx_sreg_tid_x()
429	LLVM IR:      %v5 = call i32 @llvm.nvvm.read.ptx.sreg.tid.x()
430	PTX:          mov.u32 %r1, %tid.x;
431	```
432	
433	And `shuffle_xor`:
434	
435	```text
436	Rust:         warp::shuffle_xor(val, mask)
437	MIR:          _5 = shuffle_xor(_3, _4) -> bb2
438	Pliron MIR:   %v = nvvm.shfl_sync_bfly_i32 %val, %mask : i32
439	Pliron LLVM:  %v = call i32 @llvm_nvvm_shfl_sync_bfly_i32(i32 -1, %val, %mask, i32 31)
440	LLVM IR:      %v8 = call i32 @llvm.nvvm.shfl.sync.bfly.i32(i32 -1, %v3, %v4, i32 31) #0
441	PTX:          shfl.sync.bfly.b32 %r3, %r1, %r2, 31;
442	```
443	
444	Six representations. One Rust function call becomes one PTX instruction. The
445	intermediate steps exist so that each transformation is small, verifiable,
446	and independently testable.
447	
448	---
449	
450	## Quick-Reference Checklist
451	
452	Every file you need to touch, in order:
453	
454	1. **`cuda-device/src/<module>.rs`** -- `pub fn` with `#[inline(never)]` and
455	   `unreachable!()` body. This is the user-facing API.
456	
457	2. **`dialect-nvvm/src/ops/<module>.rs`** -- `#[pliron_op(name = "nvvm.<name>", ...)]` struct,
458	   `Verify` impl (check operand/result counts), `register()` call.
459	
460	3. **`mir-importer/src/translator/terminator/mod.rs`** -- `match` arm in
461	   `try_dispatch_intrinsic()`. Use `helpers::emit_nvvm_intrinsic()` for
462	   zero-arg intrinsics, or write a custom emitter for anything with arguments.
463	
464	4. **`mir-lower/src/convert/interface_impls.rs`** -- `MirToLlvmConversion`
465	   impl for the new op, dispatching to the converter function.
466	
467	5. **`mir-lower/src/convert/intrinsics/<module>.rs`** -- conversion logic.
468	   Use `call_intrinsic()` for LLVM intrinsics, or `inline_asm_convergent()`
469	   for inline PTX.
470	
471	6. **`llvm-export/src/export/`** -- *only if convergent*: add to
472	   `is_convergent_intrinsic()`.
473	
474	---
475	
476	## Side-by-Side Comparison
477	
478	| Stage        | `threadIdx_x` (simple) | `shuffle_xor` (complex)   |
479	| :----------- | :--------------------- | :------------------------ |
480	| cuda-device  | `fn() -> u32`          | `fn(u32, u32) -> u32`     |
481	| dialect-nvvm | 0 operands, 1 result   | 2 operands, 1 result      |
482	| mir-importer | Generic helper         | Custom emitter            |
483	| mir-lower    | `call @intrinsic()`    | `call @intrinsic(4 args)` |
484	| Convergent?  | No                     | Yes (`#0`)                |
485	| PTX          | `mov.u32 %r1, %tid.x`  | `shfl.sync.bfly.b32 ...`  |
486	
487	---
488	
489	That is the entire process. Five files, each with a clear and narrow
490	responsibility. The pattern is mechanical enough that adding a new intrinsic
491	should take about thirty minutes once you have done it once -- most of that
492	time spent reading the PTX ISA spec to figure out exactly what instruction
493	you want.
494

file.content:
# Adding New Intrinsics

So you want to teach cuda-oxide a new GPU trick. Maybe NVIDIA just shipped a new
instruction, or you need an existing PTX operation that nobody has wired up yet.
Good news: the process is mechanical. Five crates, five steps, and roughly thirty
minutes once you have done it before.

This chapter walks through the full pipeline using two real examples -- one
trivially simple, one with a few twists -- so you can see exactly what happens at
each stage.

---

## The Five-Stage Pipeline

Every GPU intrinsic follows the same path through the compiler:

```text
cuda-device          User writes:  thread::threadIdx_x()
    │
    ▼
mir-importer       Compiler sees the call, emits a `dialect-nvvm` op
    │
    ▼
dialect-nvvm       The op lives here as a verified IR node
    │
    ▼
mir-lower          Converts the `dialect-nvvm` op into an LLVM dialect op
    │
    ▼
llvm-export        Exports textual LLVM IR  →  llc turns it into PTX
```

At the `mir-lower` stage you pick one of two strategies:

| Strategy            | When to use                                                                  | Examples                               |
| :------------------ | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------------------------- |
| LLVM intrinsic call | LLVM already has a built-in for it                                           | `threadIdx_x`, warp shuffles, barriers |
| Inline PTX assembly | No LLVM intrinsic exists, or you need exact control over the PTX instruction | `trap`, `wgmma`, `tcgen05`, `mbarrier` |

Both strategies are demonstrated below.

---

## Example 1: `threadIdx_x` (the Simple Case)

`threadIdx_x()` is the "Hello, World" of GPU intrinsics: zero arguments, one
`u32` result, maps directly to a single LLVM NVVM intrinsic. If you can follow
this example, you can add any simple intrinsic.

### Stage 1 -- Declare in `cuda-device`

**File:** `crates/cuda-device/src/thread.rs`

```rust
#[inline(never)]
pub fn threadIdx_x() -> u32 {
    unreachable!("threadIdx_x called outside CUDA kernel context")
}
```

Two rules that might look odd until you understand the trick:

- **`#[inline(never)]`** keeps the function visible as a distinct call in MIR.
  If rustc inlined it, the compiler would see `unreachable!()` instead of a
  call it can intercept. That would be... less than helpful.

- **The body is `unreachable!()`** because this function never actually runs.
  The compiler replaces the entire call with a `dialect-nvvm` operation
  before any GPU code executes. Think of the function as a placeholder -- a
  "dear compiler, please insert a GPU instruction here" note.

```{tip}
Document what LLVM IR or PTX the intrinsic maps to in a comment above the
function. Future you (or future contributors) will thank present you.
```

### Stage 2 -- Define the `dialect-nvvm` Op

**File:** `crates/dialect-nvvm/src/ops/thread.rs`

```rust
#[pliron_op(name = "nvvm.read_ptx_sreg_tid_x", dialect = "nvvm", format)]
pub struct ReadPtxSregTidXOp;

impl Verify for ReadPtxSregTidXOp {
    fn verify(&self, ctx: &Context) -> Result<(), Error> {
        let op = &*self.get_operation().deref(ctx);
        if op.get_num_operands() != 0 {
            return verify_err!(op.loc(), "expected 0 operands");
        }
        if op.get_num_results() != 1 {
            return verify_err!(op.loc(), "expected 1 result");
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

Then register it so pliron knows the op exists:

```rust
pub(super) fn register(ctx: &mut Context) {
    ReadPtxSregTidXOp::register(ctx, ReadPtxSregTidXOp::parser_fn);
}
```

The `Verify` trait catches structural bugs early. If something accidentally
creates this op with two operands, verification fails with a clear message
instead of producing garbage PTX three stages later.

### Stage 3 -- Recognize in `mir-importer`

**File:** `crates/mir-importer/src/translator/terminator/mod.rs`

When the translator processes MIR, every function call passes through
`try_dispatch_intrinsic()`. The callee's fully qualified domain name (FQDN)
comes from `CrateDef::name()` via `extract_func_info()`, producing paths like
`cuda_device::thread::threadIdx_x`. The match checks this FQDN:

```rust
match name {
    "cuda_device::threadIdx_x" | "cuda_device::thread::threadIdx_x" => {
        Ok(Some(helpers::emit_nvvm_intrinsic(
            ctx,
            ReadPtxSregTidXOp::get_concrete_op_info(),
            destination, target, block_ptr, prev_op,
            value_map, block_map, loc,
        )?))
    }
    // ... hundreds of other intrinsics ...
}
```

We match both the re-exported name (`cuda_device::threadIdx_x`) and the full
module path (`cuda_device::thread::threadIdx_x`) so both work regardless of how
the user imports the function. The FQDN is used as-is for matching -- no `::` to
`__` conversion happens before the intrinsic check.

The `emit_nvvm_intrinsic()` helper is a generic function that works for *any*
zero-argument, single-result NVVM intrinsic. It creates the operation, stores
the result in the value map, and emits a branch to the next basic block. For
simple intrinsics, you never need to write a custom emitter.

### Stage 4 -- Lower to the LLVM dialect

**File:** `crates/mir-lower/src/convert/intrinsics/basic.rs`

The op implements the `MirToLlvmConversion` op interface. In
`convert/interface_impls.rs`, the impl dispatches to a converter function:

```rust
impl MirToLlvmConversion for ReadPtxSregTidXOp {
    fn rewrite(ctx, rewriter, op, operands_info) -> Result<()> {
        basic::convert_read_tid_x(ctx, rewriter, op, operands_info)
    }
}
```

Then `basic::convert_read_tid_x` emits the LLVM intrinsic call:

```rust
pub fn convert_read_tid_x(
    ctx: &mut Context,
    rewriter: &mut DialectConversionRewriter,
    op: Ptr<Operation>,
    _operands_info: &OperandsInfo,
) -> Result<()> {
    let intrinsic_name = "llvm_nvvm_read_ptx_sreg_tid_x";
    let i32_ty = IntegerType::get(ctx, 32, Signedness::Signless);
    let func_ty = FuncType::get(ctx, i32_ty.into(), vec![], false);

    let call_op = call_intrinsic(ctx, rewriter, intrinsic_name, func_ty, vec![])?;
    rewriter.replace_operation_with_values(ctx, op, vec![result]);
    Ok(())
}
```

```{note}
LLVM intrinsic names use dots (`llvm.nvvm.read.ptx.sreg.tid.x`), but pliron
identifiers cannot contain dots. Internally we use underscores
(`llvm_nvvm_read_ptx_sreg_tid_x`). The export stage converts them back.
```

### Stage 5 -- Export (Nothing to Change)

**File:** `crates/llvm-export/src/export/` (the export module)

The `CallOp` exporter already handles the underscore-to-dot conversion:

```rust
let fixed_name = if name.starts_with("llvm_nvvm") {
    name.replace('_', ".")
} else {
    strip_device_prefix(&name)
};
```

Since `threadIdx_x` is *not* convergent (it is a per-thread register read,
not a collective operation), there is nothing to add to
`is_convergent_intrinsic()`.

**Final output:**

```llvm
declare i32 @llvm.nvvm.read.ptx.sreg.tid.x()
%v5 = call i32 @llvm.nvvm.read.ptx.sreg.tid.x()
```

After `llc`:

```text
mov.u32  %r1, %tid.x;
```

Five stages, one PTX instruction. Not bad.

---

## Example 2: `shuffle_xor` (the Complex Case)

Warp shuffles are more interesting. The user passes two arguments, but the
underlying LLVM intrinsic expects *four*. The compiler quietly fills in the
extras. The operation is also **convergent**, meaning LLVM must not move,
duplicate, or speculate it across control flow.

### Stage 1 -- Declare in `cuda-device`

**File:** `crates/cuda-device/src/warp.rs`

```rust
#[inline(never)]
pub fn shuffle_xor(var: u32, lane_mask: u32) -> u32 {
    let _ = (var, lane_mask);
    unreachable!("shuffle_xor called outside CUDA kernel context")
}
```

Same pattern as before. The `let _ = (var, lane_mask);` suppresses
unused-variable warnings -- a small courtesy that costs nothing.

### Stage 2 -- Define the `dialect-nvvm` Op

**File:** `crates/dialect-nvvm/src/ops/warp.rs`

```rust
#[pliron_op(name = "nvvm.shfl_sync_bfly_i32", dialect = "nvvm", format)]
pub struct ShflSyncBflyI32Op;

impl Verify for ShflSyncBflyI32Op {
    fn verify(&self, ctx: &Context) -> Result<(), Error> {
        let op = &*self.get_operation().deref(ctx);
        if op.get_num_operands() != 2 {
            return verify_err!(op.loc(), "expected 2 operands");
        }
        if op.get_num_results() != 1 {
            return verify_err!(op.loc(), "expected 1 result");
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

Two operands this time (value and lane mask), one result.

### Stage 3 -- Recognize in `mir-importer`

**File:** `crates/mir-importer/src/translator/terminator/mod.rs`

Unlike `threadIdx_x`, this calls a **specialized emitter** because it needs to
handle the user's two arguments:

```rust
"cuda_device::warp::shuffle_xor" => Ok(Some(
    intrinsics::warp::emit_warp_shuffle_i32(
        ctx, body,
        ShflSyncBflyI32Op::get_concrete_op_info(),
        args, destination, target, ...
    )?
)),
```

**File:** `crates/mir-importer/src/translator/terminator/intrinsics/warp.rs`

```rust
pub fn emit_warp_shuffle_i32(ctx, body, shuffle_opid, args, ...) {
    if args.len() != 2 { return error; }

    let (val, _) = translate_operand(ctx, body, &args[0], ...);
    let (lane_or_mask, _) = translate_operand(ctx, body, &args[1], ...);

    let shuffle_op = Operation::new(ctx, shuffle_opid,
        vec![u32_type.to_ptr()],          // results: [u32]
        vec![val, lane_or_mask],          // operands: [value, mask]
        vec![], 0,
    );

}
```

The emitter translates the user's MIR operands into pliron values --
typically the results of `mir.load`s from each operand's alloca slot, or
`mir.constant`s for literal arguments -- and wires them into the NVVM
operation. (These are not SSA values yet; `pliron::opts::mem2reg` will
collapse the load/store chains once translation is complete.) For intrinsics
with different argument patterns, you write a similar custom emitter.

### Stage 4 -- Lower to the LLVM dialect

**File:** `crates/mir-lower/src/convert/intrinsics/warp.rs`

Here is where it gets interesting. The LLVM intrinsic for butterfly shuffle
takes **four** arguments, not two:

```text
User's API:     shuffle_xor(value, lane_mask)            →  2 args
LLVM intrinsic: shfl.sync.bfly.i32(mask, value, lane_mask, clamp) →  4 args
                                    ^^^^                   ^^^^^
                                    always -1              always 31
```

The converter adds the compiler-supplied constants:

```rust
fn convert_shuffle_i32(op, ctx, intrinsic_name, clamp: i32) {
    let operands = get_operands(op, ctx)?;
    let (val, lane_or_delta) = (operands[0], operands[1]);

    let mask_val  = create_i32_const(ctx, -1);   // all 32 lanes participate
    let clamp_val = create_i32_const(ctx, clamp); // full warp width

    let func_ty = FuncType::get(ctx.ctx, i32_ty.into(),
        vec![i32_ty, i32_ty, i32_ty, i32_ty], false);

    let call_op = call_intrinsic(ctx, intrinsic_name, func_ty,
        vec![mask_val, val, lane_or_delta, clamp_val]);

    map_result(op, call_op, ctx);
}
```

The mask value of `-1` (all bits set) means all 32 lanes in the warp
participate. The clamp value of `31` means the shuffle wraps at the full warp
width. These are the right defaults for almost every use case, and exposing
them to the user would just be noise.

### Stage 5 -- Export (Add Convergent)

Warp shuffles are **convergent** -- LLVM must not reorder them relative to
control flow. The export step checks `is_convergent_intrinsic()`:

```rust
fn is_convergent_intrinsic(name: &str) -> bool {
    name == "llvm.nvvm.barrier0"
        || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.shfl")      // shuffles
        || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.vote")       // votes
        || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.mbarrier")   // async barriers
        || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.cp.async.bulk")
        // ...
}
```

If your new intrinsic is convergent, add it here. If you forget, LLVM might
hoist it out of an `if` block, and your warp-level code will produce wrong
results or deadlock. Not great.

**Final output:**

```llvm
declare i32 @llvm.nvvm.shfl.sync.bfly.i32(i32, i32, i32, i32)

%v8 = call i32 @llvm.nvvm.shfl.sync.bfly.i32(
    i32 -1, i32 %v3, i32 %v4, i32 31) #0

attributes #0 = { convergent }
```

After `llc`:

```text
shfl.sync.bfly.b32  %r3, %r1, %r2, 31;
```

---

## The Inline PTX Path

Some operations do not have LLVM intrinsics. For those, we emit inline PTX
assembly directly. The helper `inline_asm_convergent()` handles the boilerplate:

```rust
// wgmma fence: no inputs, no outputs, just a side effect
inline_asm_convergent(
    ctx, void_ty.into(), vec![],
    "wgmma.fence.sync.aligned;", ""
);

// mbarrier arrive: one input (pointer), one output (token)
inline_asm_convergent(
    ctx, i64_ty.into(), vec![ptr_val],
    "mbarrier.arrive.shared.b64 $0, [$1];", "=l,r"
);
```

The constraint string follows LLVM inline assembly syntax:

| Constraint | Meaning                                 |
| :--------- | :-------------------------------------- |
| `=l`       | Output: 64-bit register                 |
| `=r`       | Output: 32-bit register                 |
| `r`        | Input: 32-bit register                  |
| `l`        | Input: 64-bit register                  |
| (empty)    | No inputs or outputs (side-effect only) |

The `sideeffect convergent` markers on the inline assembly tell LLVM to leave
it alone -- do not move it, do not delete it, do not duplicate it.

---

## End-to-End: The Full Journey

Here is every representation `threadIdx_x` passes through, top to bottom:

```text
Rust:         thread::threadIdx_x()
MIR:          _3 = threadIdx_x() -> bb1
Pliron MIR:   %v = nvvm.read_ptx_sreg_tid_x : i32
Pliron LLVM:  %v = call i32 @llvm_nvvm_read_ptx_sreg_tid_x()
LLVM IR:      %v5 = call i32 @llvm.nvvm.read.ptx.sreg.tid.x()
PTX:          mov.u32 %r1, %tid.x;
```

And `shuffle_xor`:

```text
Rust:         warp::shuffle_xor(val, mask)
MIR:          _5 = shuffle_xor(_3, _4) -> bb2
Pliron MIR:   %v = nvvm.shfl_sync_bfly_i32 %val, %mask : i32
Pliron LLVM:  %v = call i32 @llvm_nvvm_shfl_sync_bfly_i32(i32 -1, %val, %mask, i32 31)
LLVM IR:      %v8 = call i32 @llvm.nvvm.shfl.sync.bfly.i32(i32 -1, %v3, %v4, i32 31) #0
PTX:          shfl.sync.bfly.b32 %r3, %r1, %r2, 31;
```

Six representations. One Rust function call becomes one PTX instruction. The
intermediate steps exist so that each transformation is small, verifiable,
and independently testable.

---

## Quick-Reference Checklist

Every file you need to touch, in order:

1. **`cuda-device/src/<module>.rs`** -- `pub fn` with `#[inline(never)]` and
   `unreachable!()` body. This is the user-facing API.

2. **`dialect-nvvm/src/ops/<module>.rs`** -- `#[pliron_op(name = "nvvm.<name>", ...)]` struct,
   `Verify` impl (check operand/result counts), `register()` call.

3. **`mir-importer/src/translator/terminator/mod.rs`** -- `match` arm in
   `try_dispatch_intrinsic()`. Use `helpers::emit_nvvm_intrinsic()` for
   zero-arg intrinsics, or write a custom emitter for anything with arguments.

4. **`mir-lower/src/convert/interface_impls.rs`** -- `MirToLlvmConversion`
   impl for the new op, dispatching to the converter function.

5. **`mir-lower/src/convert/intrinsics/<module>.rs`** -- conversion logic.
   Use `call_intrinsic()` for LLVM intrinsics, or `inline_asm_convergent()`
   for inline PTX.

6. **`llvm-export/src/export/`** -- *only if convergent*: add to
   `is_convergent_intrinsic()`.

---

## Side-by-Side Comparison

| Stage        | `threadIdx_x` (simple) | `shuffle_xor` (complex)   |
| :----------- | :--------------------- | :------------------------ |
| cuda-device  | `fn() -> u32`          | `fn(u32, u32) -> u32`     |
| dialect-nvvm | 0 operands, 1 result   | 2 operands, 1 result      |
| mir-importer | Generic helper         | Custom emitter            |
| mir-lower    | `call @intrinsic()`    | `call @intrinsic(4 args)` |
| Convergent?  | No                     | Yes (`#0`)                |
| PTX          | `mov.u32 %r1, %tid.x`  | `shfl.sync.bfly.b32 ...`  |

---

That is the entire process. Five files, each with a clear and narrow
responsibility. The pattern is mechanical enough that adding a new intrinsic
should take about thirty minutes once you have done it once -- most of that
time spent reading the PTX ISA spec to figure out exactly what instruction
you want.

6	//! Warp-level matrix operations.
7	//!
8	//! This module provides register-only matrix operations (`movmatrix` and
9	//! `mma.sync`) plus warp-cooperative shared-memory loads (`ldmatrix`).
10	//!
11	//! For ldmatrix, each group of four lanes loads one naturally aligned
12	//! 16-byte row:
13	//!
14	//! ```text
15	//! x1: lanes  0..7  provide addresses
16	//! x2: lanes  0..15 provide addresses
17	//! x4: lanes  0..31 provide addresses
18	//! ```
19	//!
20	//! On sm_75, x1 and x2 still require valid addresses in all 32 lanes. A
21	//! common choice is to copy the lower-lane addresses into the upper lanes.
22	//! The .trans forms use column-major rather than row-major layout.
23	//!
24	//! Ldmatrix is a weak memory operation: .sync converges the warp but does not
25	//! order memory. Callers need an appropriate barrier or fence around dependent
26	//! memory accesses. Movmatrix and MMA are register-only and have no memory
27	//! effect.
28	
29	/// Transpose an 8×8 matrix of b16 elements in-register across the warp.
30	///
31	/// Each lane provides one `u32` that packs two b16 elements of the source
32	/// matrix. The instruction collectively transposes the 8×8 tile and writes
33	/// the transposed pair back into each lane's destination register.
34	///
35	/// ```text
36	/// input  lane 4*r + k: [matrix[r][2*k], matrix[r][2*k + 1]]
37	/// output lane 4*c + k: [matrix[2*k][c], matrix[2*k + 1][c]]
38	/// ```
39	///
40	/// This operation only exchanges register fragments between lanes. It does
41	/// not access memory and is not a memory fence.
42	///
43	/// # PTX
44	///
45	/// `movmatrix.sync.aligned.m8n8.trans.b16 %d, %a;`
46	///
47	/// # Safety
48	///
49	/// - All 32 lanes must execute the same call together.
50	/// - Calling from divergent control flow is undefined behavior.
51	/// - Requires `sm_75+` and PTX ISA 7.8+. cuda-oxide selects both floors
52	///   automatically, including when targeting Turing or Ampere.
53	#[inline(never)]
54	#[must_use]
55	pub unsafe fn movmatrix_trans_b16(a: u32) -> u32 {
56	    let _ = a;
57	    unreachable!("movmatrix_trans_b16 called outside CUDA kernel context")
58	}
59	
60	// =============================================================================
61	// Shared-memory matrix loads
62	// =============================================================================
63	
64	/// Load one 8×8 matrix tile from shared memory.
65	///
66	/// # PTX
67	///
68	/// `ldmatrix.sync.aligned.m8n8.x1.shared.b16 {%r0}, [addr];`
69	///
70	/// # Safety
71	///
72	/// - Lanes 0-7 must each provide a valid, naturally aligned 16-byte shared-memory row
73	/// - On sm_75, all 32 lanes must provide a valid address
74	/// - Must be called by all threads in a warp (warp-synchronous)
75	/// - Callers must use a suitable barrier or fence to order other memory accesses
76	/// - Requires sm_75+ (Turing and later)
77	#[inline(never)]
78	pub unsafe fn ldmatrix_x1(smem_ptr: *const u32) -> u32 {
79	    let _ = smem_ptr;
80	    unreachable!("ldmatrix_x1 called outside CUDA kernel context")
81	}
82	
83	/// Load one 8×8 matrix tile from shared memory in column-major layout.
84	///
85	/// # PTX
86	///
87	/// `ldmatrix.sync.aligned.m8n8.x1.trans.shared.b16 {%r0}, [addr];`
88	///
89	/// # Safety
90	///
91	/// Same address-lane, synchronization, and target requirements as [`ldmatrix_x1`].
92	#[inline(never)]
93	pub unsafe fn ldmatrix_x1_trans(smem_ptr: *const u32) -> u32 {
94	    let _ = smem_ptr;
95	    unreachable!("ldmatrix_x1_trans called outside CUDA kernel context")
96	}
97	
98	/// Load 2 packed 8×8 matrices from shared memory.
99	///
100	/// Returns `[u32; 2]` (each u32 = 2 packed b16 values).
101	///
102	/// # PTX
103	///
104	/// `ldmatrix.sync.aligned.m8n8.x2.shared.b16 {%r0, %r1}, [addr];`
105	///
106	/// # Safety
107	///
108	/// - Lanes 0-15 provide the 16 row addresses
109	/// - On sm_75, all 32 lanes must provide a valid address
110	/// - All lanes must participate, and callers must order other memory accesses
111	/// - Requires sm_75+ (Turing and later)
112	#[inline(never)]
113	pub unsafe fn ldmatrix_x2(smem_ptr: *const u32) -> [u32; 2] {
114	    let _ = smem_ptr;
115	    unreachable!("ldmatrix_x2 called outside CUDA kernel context")
116	}
117	
118	/// Load 2 packed 8×8 matrices from shared memory in column-major layout.
119	///
120	/// # PTX
121	///
122	/// `ldmatrix.sync.aligned.m8n8.x2.trans.shared.b16 {%r0, %r1}, [addr];`
123	///
124	/// # Safety
125	///
126	/// Same address-lane, synchronization, and target requirements as [`ldmatrix_x2`].
127	#[inline(never)]
128	pub unsafe fn ldmatrix_x2_trans(smem_ptr: *const u32) -> [u32; 2] {
129	    let _ = smem_ptr;
130	    unreachable!("ldmatrix_x2_trans called outside CUDA kernel context")
131	}
132	
133	/// Load 4 packed 8×8 matrices from shared memory.
134	///
135	/// Returns `[u32; 4]` (each u32 = 2 packed b16 values).
136	///
137	/// # PTX
138	///
139	/// `ldmatrix.sync.aligned.m8n8.x4.shared.b16 {%r0, %r1, %r2, %r3}, [addr];`
140	///
141	/// # Safety
142	///
143	/// - All 32 lanes provide valid, naturally aligned 16-byte row addresses
144	/// - All lanes must participate, and callers must order other memory accesses
145	/// - Requires sm_75+ (Turing and later)
146	#[inline(never)]
147	pub unsafe fn ldmatrix_x4(smem_ptr: *const u32) -> [u32; 4] {
148	    let _ = smem_ptr;
149	    unreachable!("ldmatrix_x4 called outside CUDA kernel context")
150	}
151	
152	/// Load 4 packed 8×8 matrices from shared memory in column-major layout.
153	///
154	/// # PTX
155	///
156	/// `ldmatrix.sync.aligned.m8n8.x4.trans.shared.b16 {%r0, %r1, %r2, %r3}, [addr];`
157	///
158	/// # Safety
159	///
160	/// Same address-lane, synchronization, and target requirements as [`ldmatrix_x4`].
161	#[inline(never)]
162	pub unsafe fn ldmatrix_x4_trans(smem_ptr: *const u32) -> [u32; 4] {
163	    let _ = smem_ptr;
164	    unreachable!("ldmatrix_x4_trans called outside CUDA kernel context")
165	}
166	
167	/// Multiply one warp-distributed BF16 tile and add an f32 accumulator.
168	///
169	/// Together, the 32 lanes compute `D = A × B + C` for row-major `A` with
170	/// shape 16×16, column-major `B` with shape 16×8, and `C`/`D` with shape
171	/// 16×8. Each lane supplies its fragments in registers and receives four f32
172	/// result registers. The call itself does not access memory or act as a fence.
173	///
174	/// `a[j / 2]` and `b[j / 2]` pack logical element `j` in low-to-high 16-bit
175	/// order. For lane `lane`, let `group = lane / 4` and `thread = lane % 4`:
176	///
177	/// ```text
178	/// A element j=0..7:
179	///   row = group       for j in {0,1,4,5}; otherwise group + 8
180	///   col = thread*2 + (j&1) + (if j >= 4 { 8 } else { 0 })
181	///
182	/// B element j=0..3:
183	///   row = thread*2 + (j&1) + (if j >= 2 { 8 } else { 0 })
184	///   col = group
185	///
186	/// C/D register j=0..3:
187	///   row = group + (if j >= 2 { 8 } else { 0 })
188	///   col = thread*2 + (j&1)
189	/// ```
190	///
191	/// # PTX
192	///
193	/// ```ptx
194	/// mma.sync.aligned.m16n8k16.row.col.f32.bf16.bf16.f32
195	///     {%d0, %d1, %d2, %d3},
196	///     {%a0, %a1, %a2, %a3},
197	///     {%b0, %b1},
198	///     {%c0, %c1, %c2, %c3};
199	/// ```
200	///
201	/// # Safety
202	///
203	/// - All 32 lanes must execute the same call with the same qualifiers. Calling
204	///   from divergent control flow, or after any lane has exited, is undefined
205	///   behavior.
206	/// - `c`, `a`, and `b` must contain the calling lane's fragments in the layout
207	///   above. A different layout computes a different matrix operation.
208	/// - Requires `sm_80+` and PTX ISA 7.0+. cuda-oxide selects both floors
209	///   automatically and rejects an explicit lower target.
210	#[inline(never)]
211	#[must_use]
212	pub unsafe fn mma_m16n8k16_f32_bf16(c: [f32; 4], a: [u32; 4], b: [u32; 2]) -> [f32; 4] {
213	    let _ = (c, a, b);
214	    unreachable!("mma_m16n8k16_f32_bf16 called outside CUDA kernel context")
215	}
216	
217	/// Multiply one warp-distributed F16 tile and add an f32 accumulator.
218	///
219	/// Together, the 32 lanes compute `D = A × B + C` for row-major `A` with
220	/// shape 16×16, column-major `B` with shape 16×8, and `C`/`D` with shape
221	/// 16×8. Each lane supplies its fragments in registers and receives four f32
222	/// result registers. The call itself does not access memory or act as a fence.
223	///
224	/// The Rust arrays use the same order as the PTX register lists below:
225	///
226	/// - `c[0..4]` contains `%c0..%c3`, one `.f32` accumulator per element. The
227	///   returned array contains `%d0..%d3` in the same order.
228	/// - `a[0..4]` contains `%a0..%a3`, and `b[0..2]` contains `%b0..%b1`.
229	///   Each `u32` is a raw `.b32` carrier holding two `.f16` values; element
230	///   `j` is in `a[j / 2]` or `b[j / 2]`, low 16 bits before high 16 bits.
231	///
232	/// For lane `lane`, let `group = lane / 4` and `thread = lane % 4`:
233	///
234	/// ```text
235	/// A element j=0..7:
236	///   row = group       for j in {0,1,4,5}; otherwise group + 8
237	///   col = thread*2 + (j&1) + (if j >= 4 { 8 } else { 0 })
238	///
239	/// B element j=0..3:
240	///   row = thread*2 + (j&1) + (if j >= 2 { 8 } else { 0 })
241	///   col = group
242	///
243	/// C/D register j=0..3:
244	///   row = group + (if j >= 2 { 8 } else { 0 })
245	///   col = thread*2 + (j&1)
246	/// ```
247	///
248	/// These coordinates are the `.row.col` layout: A is row-major and B is
249	/// column-major.
250	///
251	/// # PTX
252	///
253	/// ```ptx
254	/// mma.sync.aligned.m16n8k16.row.col.f32.f16.f16.f32
255	///     {%d0, %d1, %d2, %d3},
256	///     {%a0, %a1, %a2, %a3},
257	///     {%b0, %b1},
258	///     {%c0, %c1, %c2, %c3};
259	/// ```
260	///
261	/// # Safety
262	///
263	/// - All 32 lanes must execute the same `mma.sync.aligned` instruction with
264	///   the same qualifiers. Calling from divergent control flow, or after any
265	///   lane has exited, is undefined behavior.
266	/// - `c`, `a`, and `b` must contain the calling lane's fragments in the layout
267	///   above. A different array order or layout computes a different matrix
268	///   operation.
269	/// - Requires `sm_80+` and PTX ISA 7.0+. cuda-oxide selects both floors
270	///   automatically and rejects an explicit lower target.
271	#[inline(never)]
272	#[must_use]
273	pub unsafe fn mma_m16n8k16_f32_f16(c: [f32; 4], a: [u32; 4], b: [u32; 2]) -> [f32; 4] {
274	    let _ = (c, a, b);
275	    unreachable!("mma_m16n8k16_f32_f16 called outside CUDA kernel context")
276	}
277	
278	/// Multiply one warp-distributed TF32 tile and add an f32 accumulator.
279	///
280	/// Together, the 32 lanes compute `D = A × B + C` for row-major `A` with
281	/// shape 16×8, column-major `B` with shape 8×8, and `C`/`D` with shape
282	/// 16×8. Each lane supplies its fragments in registers and receives four f32
283	/// result registers. The call itself does not access memory or act as a fence.
284	///
285	/// The Rust arrays use the same order as the PTX register lists below:
286	///
287	/// - `c[0..4]` contains `%c0..%c3`, one `.f32` accumulator per element. The
288	///   returned array contains `%d0..%d3` in the same order.
289	/// - `a[0..4]` contains `%a0..%a3`, and `b[0..2]` contains `%b0..%b1`.
290	///   Each `u32` is a raw `.b32` register containing one TF32 value.
291	///
292	/// For lane `lane`, let `group = lane / 4` and `thread = lane % 4`:
293	///
294	/// ```text
295	/// A register j=0..3:
296	///   row = group + (if j in {1,3} { 8 } else { 0 })
297	///   col = thread + (if j >= 2 { 4 } else { 0 })
298	///
299	/// B register j=0..1:
300	///   row = thread + (if j == 1 { 4 } else { 0 })
301	///   col = group
302	///
303	/// C/D register j=0..3:
304	///   row = group + (if j >= 2 { 8 } else { 0 })
305	///   col = thread*2 + (j&1)
306	/// ```
307	///
308	/// These coordinates are the `.row.col` layout: A is row-major and B is
309	/// column-major. The raw A and B registers must already contain valid TF32
310	/// values produced by `cvt.rna.tf32.f32` or an equivalent TF32-producing
311	/// operation. Passing arbitrary `f32::to_bits()` values is not a conversion to
312	/// TF32 and does not satisfy this contract.
313	///
314	/// # PTX
315	///
316	/// ```ptx
317	/// mma.sync.aligned.m16n8k8.row.col.f32.tf32.tf32.f32
318	///     {%d0, %d1, %d2, %d3},
319	///     {%a0, %a1, %a2, %a3},
320	///     {%b0, %b1},
321	///     {%c0, %c1, %c2, %c3};
322	/// ```
323	///
324	/// # Safety
325	///
326	/// - All 32 lanes must execute the same `mma.sync.aligned` instruction with
327	///   the same qualifiers. Calling from divergent control flow, or after any
328	///   lane has exited, is undefined behavior.
329	/// - `c`, `a`, and `b` must contain the calling lane's fragments in the array
330	///   order and layout above. A different order computes a different matrix
331	///   operation; invalid TF32 bit patterns do not become valid merely because
332	///   they are carried in `u32`.
333	/// - Requires `sm_80+` and PTX ISA 7.0+. cuda-oxide selects both floors
334	///   automatically and rejects an explicit lower target.
335	#[inline(never)]
336	#[must_use]
337	pub unsafe fn mma_m16n8k8_f32_tf32(c: [f32; 4], a: [u32; 4], b: [u32; 2]) -> [f32; 4] {
338	    let _ = (c, a, b);
339	    unreachable!("mma_m16n8k8_f32_tf32 called outside CUDA kernel context")
340	}
341	
342	/// Warp MMA: D = A x B + C (m8n8k4, f64 output, f64 inputs).
343	///
344	/// Performs an 8x8x4 double-precision matrix multiplication using tensor cores.
345	/// All 32 threads in the warp participate.
346	///
347	/// # Matrix Dimensions
348	///
349	/// - **A**: 8x4 (row-major, f64), distributed as 1 x f64 per thread
350	/// - **B**: 4x8 (col-major, f64), distributed as 1 x f64 per thread
351	/// - **D/C**: 8x8 (f64 accumulator), distributed as 2 x f64 per thread
352	///
353	/// Each lane owns these matrix elements:
354	///
355	/// ```text
356	/// A:   row = lane / 4, column = lane % 4
357	/// B:   row = lane % 4, column = lane / 4
358	/// C/D: row = lane / 4, columns = 2 * (lane % 4) + {0, 1}
359	/// ```
360	///
361	/// `acc` contains the lane's two C-fragment registers. The return value is
362	/// the lane's two D-fragment registers. `a` and `b` are the lane's single
363	/// multiplicand registers.
364	///
365	/// The multiply-add uses round-to-nearest-even (`.rn`) rounding.
366	///
367	/// # PTX
368	///
369	/// ```ptx
370	/// mma.sync.aligned.m8n8k4.row.col.f64.f64.f64.f64
371	///     {%d0, %d1},
372	///     {%a0},
373	///     {%b0},
374	///     {%c0, %c1};
375	/// ```
376	///
377	/// # Safety
378	///
379	/// - All 32 lanes in the warp must execute the same call together.
380	/// - Calling from divergent control flow is undefined behavior.
381	/// - No participating lane may have exited before the call.
382	/// - Requires `sm_80+` and PTX ISA 7.0+. cuda-oxide selects both floors
383	///   automatically.
384	#[inline(never)]
385	#[must_use]
386	pub unsafe fn mma_m8n8k4_f64(acc: [f64; 2], a: f64, b: f64) -> [f64; 2] {
387	    let _ = (acc, a, b);
388	    unreachable!("mma_m8n8k4_f64 called outside CUDA kernel context")
389	}
390	
391	/// Multiply one warp-distributed INT8 tile and add an i32 accumulator.
392	///
393	/// Together, the 32 lanes compute `D = A × B + C` for row-major `A` with
394	/// shape 16×32, column-major `B` with shape 32×8, and `C`/`D` with shape
395	/// 16×8. Each lane supplies its fragments in registers and receives four i32
396	/// result registers. The call itself does not access memory or act as a fence.
397	///
398	/// Each `a` and `b` register packs four two's-complement `s8` values from low
399	/// byte to high byte. In other words, logical element `i` is stored in bits
400	/// `(i % 4) * 8..(i % 4 + 1) * 8` of register `[i / 4]`.
401	///
402	/// For lane `lane`, let `group = lane / 4` and `thread = lane % 4`:
403	///
404	/// ```text
405	/// A element i=0,...,15:
406	///   row = group       for 0 <= i < 4 or 8 <= i < 12; otherwise group + 8
407	///   col = thread*4 + (i&3) + (if i >= 8 { 16 } else { 0 })
408	///
409	/// B element i=0,...,7:
410	///   row = thread*4 + (i&3) + (if i >= 4 { 16 } else { 0 })
411	///   col = group
412	///
413	/// C/D register i=0,...,3:
414	///   row = group + (if i >= 2 { 8 } else { 0 })
415	///   col = thread*2 + (i&1)
416	/// ```
417	///
418	/// This is the non-`.satfinite` form. Signed accumulator overflow wraps rather
419	/// than clamping to `i32::MIN` or `i32::MAX`.
420	///
421	/// # PTX
422	///
423	/// ```ptx
424	/// mma.sync.aligned.m16n8k32.row.col.s32.s8.s8.s32
425	///     {%d0, %d1, %d2, %d3},
426	///     {%a0, %a1, %a2, %a3},
427	///     {%b0, %b1},
428	///     {%c0, %c1, %c2, %c3};
429	/// ```
430	///
431	/// # Safety
432	///
433	/// - All 32 lanes must execute the same instruction with the same qualifiers.
434	///   Calling from divergent control flow, or after any lane has exited, is
435	///   undefined behavior.
436	/// - `c`, `a`, and `b` must contain the calling lane's fragments in the layout
437	///   above. A different layout computes a different matrix operation.
438	/// - Requires `sm_80+` and PTX ISA 7.0+. cuda-oxide selects both floors
439	///   automatically and rejects an explicit lower target.
440	#[inline(never)]
441	#[must_use]
442	pub unsafe fn mma_m16n8k32_s32_s8(c: [i32; 4], a: [u32; 4], b: [u32; 2]) -> [i32; 4] {
443	    let _ = (c, a, b);
444	    unreachable!("mma_m16n8k32_s32_s8 called outside CUDA kernel context")
445	}

6	//! Warp-level matrix intrinsics (`movmatrix`, `mma.sync`).
7	
8	use super::super::helpers::emit_store_result_and_goto;
9	use crate::error::{TranslationErr, TranslationResult};
10	use crate::translator::rvalue;
11	use crate::translator::values::ValueMap;
12	use dialect_mir::{
13	    attributes::FieldIndexAttr,
14	    ops::{MirConstructArrayOp, MirExtractFieldOp},
15	    types::MirArrayType,
16	};
17	use dialect_nvvm::ops::{
18	    MmaM8N8K4F64Op, MmaM16N8K8F32Tf32Op, MmaM16N8K16F32Bf16Op, MmaM16N8K16F32F16Op,
19	    MmaM16N8K32S32S8Op, MovmatrixTransB16Op,
20	};
21	use pliron::basic_block::BasicBlock;
22	use pliron::builtin::types::{FP32Type, FP64Type, IntegerType, Signedness};
23	use pliron::context::{Context, Ptr};
24	use pliron::input_err;
25	use pliron::location::{Located, Location};
26	use pliron::op::Op;

29	use pliron::value::Value;
30	use rustc_public::mir;
31	
32	/// Emit movmatrix_trans_b16: in-register 8×8 matrix transpose.
33	///
34	/// Takes one u32 operand and returns one u32.
35	#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]
36	pub fn emit_movmatrix_trans_b16(
37	    ctx: &mut Context,
38	    body: &mir::Body,
39	    args: &[mir::Operand],
40	    destination: &mir::Place,
41	    target: &Option<usize>,
42	    block_ptr: Ptr<BasicBlock>,
43	    prev_op: Option<Ptr<Operation>>,
44	    value_map: &mut ValueMap,
45	    block_map: &[Ptr<BasicBlock>],
46	    loc: Location,
47	) -> TranslationResult<Ptr<Operation>> {
48	    if args.len() != 1 {
49	        return input_err!(
50	            loc.clone(),
51	            TranslationErr::unsupported(format!(
52	                "movmatrix_trans_b16 expects 1 argument, got {}",
53	                args.len()
54	            ))
55	        );
56	    }
57	
58	    let (a_val, last_op) = rvalue::translate_operand(
59	        ctx,
60	        body,
61	        &args[0],
62	        value_map,
63	        block_ptr,
64	        prev_op,
65	        loc.clone(),
66	    )?;
67	
68	    let u32_ty = IntegerType::get(ctx, 32, Signedness::Unsigned);
69	
70	    let mov_op = Operation::new(
71	        ctx,
72	        MovmatrixTransB16Op::get_concrete_op_info(),

74	        vec![a_val],
75	        vec![],
76	        0,
77	    );
78	    mov_op.deref_mut(ctx).set_loc(loc.clone());
79	
80	    if let Some(prev) = last_op {
81	        mov_op.insert_after(ctx, prev);
82	    } else {
83	        mov_op.insert_at_front(block_ptr, ctx);
84	    }
85	
86	    let result = mov_op.deref(ctx).get_result(0);
87	    emit_store_result_and_goto(
88	        ctx,
89	        destination,
90	        result,
91	        target,
92	        block_ptr,
93	        mov_op,
94	        value_map,
95	        block_map,
96	        loc,
97	        "movmatrix_trans_b16 call without target block",
98	    )

101	/// Extract a fixed-size Rust array into scalar SSA register values.
102	///
103	/// Constant-field extraction lowers to LLVM `extractvalue`, so no temporary
104	/// stack slot is introduced for the MMA fragments.
105	fn extract_array_registers(
106	    ctx: &mut Context,
107	    array: Value,
108	    expected_element_ty: TypeHandle,
109	    expected_len: usize,
110	    block_ptr: Ptr<BasicBlock>,
111	    mut last_op: Option<Ptr<Operation>>,
112	    loc: Location,
113	    fragment_name: &str,
114	) -> TranslationResult<(Vec<Value>, Ptr<Operation>)> {
115	    let array_ty = array.get_type(ctx);
116	    let valid_array = {
117	        let array_ty = array_ty.deref(ctx);
118	        array_ty
119	            .downcast_ref::<MirArrayType>()
120	            .is_some_and(|array_ty| {
121	                array_ty.size() == expected_len as u64
122	                    && array_ty.element_type() == expected_element_ty
123	            })
124	    };
125	    if !valid_array {
126	        return input_err!(
127	            loc,
128	            TranslationErr::unsupported(format!(
129	                "MMA {fragment_name} fragment must be an array of {expected_len} scalar registers"
130	            ))
131	        );
132	    }
133	
134	    let mut registers = Vec::with_capacity(expected_len);
135	    for index in 0..expected_len {
136	        let extract = Operation::new(
137	            ctx,
138	            MirExtractFieldOp::get_concrete_op_info(),
139	            vec![expected_element_ty],
140	            vec![array],
141	            vec![],
142	            0,
143	        );
144	        extract.deref_mut(ctx).set_loc(loc.clone());
145	        let extract = MirExtractFieldOp::new(extract);
146	        extract.set_attr_index(ctx, FieldIndexAttr(index as u32));
147	        if let Some(previous) = last_op {
148	            extract.get_operation().insert_after(ctx, previous);
149	        } else {
150	            extract.get_operation().insert_at_front(block_ptr, ctx);
151	        }
152	        last_op = Some(extract.get_operation());
153	        registers.push(extract.get_operation().deref(ctx).get_result(0));
154	    }
155	
156	    Ok((registers, last_op.expect("non-empty MMA fragments")))
157	}

159	/// Emit `mma_m16n8k16_f32_bf16` as a register-producing dialect operation.
160	///
161	/// Args:
162	/// - `args[0]`: `[f32; 4]` C accumulator registers
163	/// - `args[1]`: `[u32; 4]` packed A fragment registers
164	/// - `args[2]`: `[u32; 2]` packed B fragment registers
165	///
166	/// Returns: `[f32; 4]` D accumulator registers.
167	pub fn emit_mma_m16n8k16_f32_bf16(
168	    ctx: &mut Context,
169	    body: &mir::Body,
170	    args: &[mir::Operand],
171	    destination: &mir::Place,
172	    target: &Option<usize>,
173	    block_ptr: Ptr<BasicBlock>,
174	    prev_op: Option<Ptr<Operation>>,
175	    value_map: &mut ValueMap,
176	    block_map: &[Ptr<BasicBlock>],
177	    loc: Location,
178	) -> TranslationResult<Ptr<Operation>> {
179	    if args.len() != 3 {
180	        return input_err!(
181	            loc.clone(),
182	            TranslationErr::unsupported(format!(
183	                "mma_m16n8k16_f32_bf16 expects 3 arguments (acc, a, b), got {}",
184	                args.len()
185	            ))
186	        );
187	    }
188	
189	    let f32_ty = FP32Type::get(ctx);
190	    let u32_ty = IntegerType::get(ctx, 32, Signedness::Unsigned);
191	
192	    let (c_array, last_op) = rvalue::translate_operand(
193	        ctx,
194	        body,
195	        &args[0],
196	        value_map,
197	        block_ptr,
198	        prev_op,
199	        loc.clone(),
200	    )?;
201	    let (c_registers, last_op) = extract_array_registers(
202	        ctx,
203	        c_array,
204	        f32_ty.into(),
205	        4,
206	        block_ptr,
207	        last_op,
208	        loc.clone(),
209	        "C",
210	    )?;
211	
212	    let (a_array, last_op_after) = rvalue::translate_operand(
213	        ctx,
214	        body,
215	        &args[1],
216	        value_map,
217	        block_ptr,
218	        Some(last_op),
219	        loc.clone(),
220	    )?;
221	    let (a_registers, last_op) = extract_array_registers(
222	        ctx,
223	        a_array,
224	        u32_ty.into(),
225	        4,
226	        block_ptr,
227	        last_op_after,
228	        loc.clone(),
229	        "A",
230	    )?;
231	
232	    let (b_array, last_op_after) = rvalue::translate_operand(
233	        ctx,
234	        body,
235	        &args[2],
236	        value_map,
237	        block_ptr,
238	        Some(last_op),
239	        loc.clone(),
240	    )?;
241	    let (b_registers, last_op) = extract_array_registers(
242	        ctx,
243	        b_array,
244	        u32_ty.into(),
245	        2,
246	        block_ptr,
247	        last_op_after,
248	        loc.clone(),
249	        "B",
250	    )?;
251	
252	    let mut operands = c_registers;
253	    operands.extend(a_registers);
254	    operands.extend(b_registers);
255	
256	    let mma_op = Operation::new(
257	        ctx,
258	        MmaM16N8K16F32Bf16Op::get_concrete_op_info(),
259	        vec![f32_ty.into(); 4],
260	        operands,
261	        vec![],
262	        0,
263	    );
264	    mma_op.deref_mut(ctx).set_loc(loc.clone());
265	    mma_op.insert_after(ctx, last_op);
266	
267	    let d_registers = (0..4)
268	        .map(|index| mma_op.deref(ctx).get_result(index))
269	        .collect();
270	    let array_ty = MirArrayType::get(ctx, f32_ty.into(), 4);
271	    let d_array = Operation::new(
272	        ctx,
273	        MirConstructArrayOp::get_concrete_op_info(),
274	        vec![array_ty.into()],
275	        d_registers,
276	        vec![],
277	        0,
278	    );
279	    d_array.deref_mut(ctx).set_loc(loc.clone());
280	    d_array.insert_after(ctx, mma_op);
281	    let result = d_array.deref(ctx).get_result(0);
282	
283	    emit_store_result_and_goto(
284	        ctx,
285	        destination,
286	        result,
287	        target,
288	        block_ptr,
289	        d_array,
290	        value_map,
291	        block_map,
292	        loc,
293	        "mma_m16n8k16_f32_bf16 call without target block",
294	    )
295	}

716	/// - arg 2: `f64` (lane-local B fragment)

718	/// Returns: `[f64; 2]` (lane-local D result fragment)
719	#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]
720	pub fn emit_mma_m8n8k4_f64(
721	    ctx: &mut Context,

731	) -> TranslationResult<Ptr<Operation>> {
732	    if args.len() != 3 {
733	        return input_err!(
734	            loc.clone(),
735	            TranslationErr::unsupported(format!(
736	                "mma_m8n8k4_f64 expects 3 arguments (acc, a, b), got {}",
737	                args.len()
738	            ))
739	        );
740	    }
741	
742	    let (acc, mut last_op) = rvalue::translate_operand(
743	        ctx,
744	        body,
745	        &args[0],
746	        value_map,
747	        block_ptr,
748	        prev_op,
749	        loc.clone(),
750	    )?;
751	
752	    let f64_ty = FP64Type::get(ctx);
753	    let acc_ty = acc.get_type(ctx);
754	    let valid_acc = acc_ty
755	        .deref(ctx)
756	        .downcast_ref::<MirArrayType>()
757	        .is_some_and(|array| {
758	            array.size() == 2
759	                && array
760	                    .element_type()
761	                    .deref(ctx)
762	                    .downcast_ref::<FP64Type>()
763	                    .is_some()
764	        });
765	    if !valid_acc {
766	        return input_err!(
767	            loc.clone(),
768	            TranslationErr::unsupported(
769	                "mma_m8n8k4_f64 accumulator must have type [f64; 2]".to_string()
770	            )
771	        );
772	    }
773	
774	    let mut accumulator_registers = Vec::with_capacity(2);
775	    for index in 0..2 {
776	        let extract = Operation::new(
777	            ctx,
778	            MirExtractFieldOp::get_concrete_op_info(),
779	            vec![f64_ty.into()],
780	            vec![acc],
781	            vec![],
782	            0,
783	        );
784	        extract.deref_mut(ctx).set_loc(loc.clone());
785	        MirExtractFieldOp::new(extract).set_attr_index(ctx, FieldIndexAttr(index));
786	        if let Some(previous) = last_op {
787	            extract.insert_after(ctx, previous);
788	        } else {
789	            extract.insert_at_front(block_ptr, ctx);
790	        }
791	        last_op = Some(extract);
792	        accumulator_registers.push(extract.deref(ctx).get_result(0));
793	    }
794	
795	    let (a, last_op_after) = rvalue::translate_operand(
796	        ctx,
797	        body,
798	        &args[1],
799	        value_map,
800	        block_ptr,
801	        last_op,
802	        loc.clone(),
803	    )?;
804	    last_op = last_op_after;
805	
806	    let (b, last_op_after) = rvalue::translate_operand(
807	        ctx,
808	        body,
809	        &args[2],
810	        value_map,
811	        block_ptr,
812	        last_op,
813	        loc.clone(),
814	    )?;
815	    last_op = last_op_after;
816	
817	    for (name, value) in [("A", a), ("B", b)] {
818	        if value
819	            .get_type(ctx)
820	            .deref(ctx)
821	            .downcast_ref::<FP64Type>()
822	            .is_none()
823	        {
824	            return input_err!(
825	                loc.clone(),
826	                TranslationErr::unsupported(format!(
827	                    "mma_m8n8k4_f64 {name} fragment must have type f64"
828	                ))
829	            );
830	        }
831	    }
832	
833	    let operands = vec![accumulator_registers[0], accumulator_registers[1], a, b];
834	
835	    let mma_op = Operation::new(
836	        ctx,
837	        MmaM8N8K4F64Op::get_concrete_op_info(),
838	        vec![f64_ty.into(), f64_ty.into()],
839	        operands,
840	        vec![],
841	        0,
842	    );
843	    mma_op.deref_mut(ctx).set_loc(loc.clone());
844	
845	    if let Some(prev) = last_op {
846	        mma_op.insert_after(ctx, prev);
847	    } else {
848	        mma_op.insert_at_front(block_ptr, ctx);
849	    }
850	
851	    let results: Vec<Value> = (0..2)
852	        .map(|index| mma_op.deref(ctx).get_result(index))
853	        .collect();
854	    let array_ty = MirArrayType::get(ctx, f64_ty.into(), 2);
855	    let array = Operation::new(
856	        ctx,
857	        MirConstructArrayOp::get_concrete_op_info(),
858	        vec![array_ty.into()],
859	        results,
860	        vec![],
861	        0,
862	    );
863	    array.deref_mut(ctx).set_loc(loc.clone());
864	    array.insert_after(ctx, mma_op);
865	    let array_result = array.deref(ctx).get_result(0);
866	
867	    emit_store_result_and_goto(
868	        ctx,
869	        destination,
870	        array_result,
871	        target,
872	        block_ptr,
873	        array,
874	        value_map,
875	        block_map,
876	        loc,
877	        "mma_m8n8k4_f64 call without target block",
878	    )
879	}
880	
881	#[cfg(test)]
882	mod tests {
883	    use super::*;
884	    use pliron::linked_list::ContainsLinkedList;
885	
886	    #[test]
887	    fn mma_fragments_are_extracted_as_constant_index_ssa_values() {
888	        let mut ctx = Context::new();
889	        dialect_mir::register(&mut ctx);
890	
891	        let f32_ty = FP32Type::get(&ctx);
892	        let array_ty = MirArrayType::get(&mut ctx, f32_ty.into(), 4);
893	        let block = BasicBlock::new(&mut ctx, None, vec![array_ty.into()]);
894	        let array = block.deref(&ctx).get_argument(0);
895	
896	        let (registers, last_op) = extract_array_registers(
897	            &mut ctx,
898	            array,
899	            f32_ty.into(),
900	            4,
901	            block,
902	            None,
903	            Location::Unknown,
904	            "C",
905	        )
906	        .expect("valid C fragment must extract");
907	
908	        assert_eq!(registers.len(), 4);
909	        assert!(
910	            registers
911	                .iter()
912	                .all(|register| register.get_type(&ctx) == f32_ty.into())
913	        );
914	
915	        let operations: Vec<_> = block.deref(&ctx).iter(&ctx).collect();
916	        assert_eq!(operations.len(), 4);
917	        assert_eq!(operations.last().copied(), Some(last_op));
918	        for (index, operation) in operations.into_iter().enumerate() {
919	            let extract = Operation::get_op::<MirExtractFieldOp>(operation, &ctx)
920	                .expect("fragment extraction must use constant-index extract_field");
921	            assert_eq!(
922	                extract.get_attr_index(&ctx).map(|attr| attr.0),
923	                Some(index as u32)
924	            );
925	        }
926	
927	        let message = extract_array_registers(
928	            &mut ctx,
929	            array,
930	            f32_ty.into(),
931	            2,
932	            block,
933	            Some(last_op),
934	            Location::Unknown,
935	            "B",
936	        )
937	        .expect_err("invalid fragment must report an error")
938	        .to_string();
939	        assert!(
940	            message.contains("MMA B fragment must be an array of 2 scalar registers"),
941	            "unexpected fragment diagnostic: {message}"
942	        );
943	        assert!(
944	            !message.contains("bf16")
945	                && !message.contains("tf32")
946	                && !message.contains("f16")
947	                && !message.contains("s8"),
948	            "shared fragment diagnostics must not name a different MMA operation: {message}"
949	        );
950	    }
951	}

209:    pub(super) fn is_convergent_intrinsic(name: &str) -> bool {
210-        // Block-level barriers
211-        name == "llvm.nvvm.barrier0"
212-            || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.barrier")
213-            // mbarrier operations
214-            || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.mbarrier")
215-            // Warp shuffles (though LLVM usually handles these)
216-            || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.shfl")
217-            // Warp votes
218-            || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.vote")
219-            // Warp match collectives (match.{any,all}.sync.*)
220-            || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.match")
221-            // Warp-level barrier (bar.warp.sync). Note the block-level
222-            // `barrier` prefix above does not match the `bar.` spelling.
223-            || name == "llvm.nvvm.bar.warp.sync"
224-            // Active-lane mask query; its result depends on warp convergence.
225-            || name == "llvm.nvvm.activemask"
226-            // Warp reductions (redux.sync.*)
227-            || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.redux")
228-            // Async bulk operations (TMA)
229-            || name.starts_with("llvm.nvvm.cp.async.bulk")

input.content:
# 端到端新增一个 intrinsic（以 mma 为模板）

## 1. 本讲目标

学完本讲，你应该能够：

- 说出「新增一个 GPU intrinsic」在 cuda-oxide 里要穿过哪几个 crate，以及为什么必须多层协同、缺一不可。
- 以 `mma_m16n8k16_f32_f16`（f16 张量核，本轮 #327 之前已存在）和 `mma_m16n8k32_s32_s8`（int8 张量核，本轮 #329 新增）为样板，在每个层指出「该写哪段代码、为什么这么写」。
- 独立画出一条 intrinsic 从 Rust 调用到 PTX 指令的六种表示形态。
- 拿到一条尚未接线的 PTX 指令（例如 `mma` 的 `.satfinite` 变体、某个 `redux` 归约），照模板列出完整改动清单。

本讲是 **u6-l2（mir-importer 翻译机）** 与 **u6-l3（mir-lower ops 转换）** 的合龙篇：前两讲分别深潜了「翻译」和「lowering」两个工位，本讲把它们与上下游的设备 API 层、方言层、导出层串成一条**可复制的工程流水线**。

## 2. 前置知识

本讲假设你已经读过：

- **u5-l6**：fragment（warp 分布式片段）、ldmatrix、mma.sync/wgmma/tcgen05 三代加速器的本质差异。本讲的 intrinsic 全是「寄存器 fragment 进、寄存器 fragment 出」的张量核指令。
- **u6-l2**：mir-importer 的终止符漏斗 `translate_call` → `try_dispatch_intrinsic`，以及「设备桩」概念——`#[inline(never)]` + `unreachable!()`，靠名字识别后改写为方言 op。
- **u6-l3**：mir-lower 的 `DialectConversion` 派发骨架、`MirToLlvmConversion` op 接口、`inline_asm_convergent` 与 `call_intrinsic` 两条 lowering 路径、fast-math 标志。

两个本讲会反复用到、但值得先点明的术语：

- **设备桩（device stub）**：`cuda-device` 里那些函数体只有 `unreachable!()` 的 `unsafe fn`。它们永远不会真在 GPU 上执行——编译器会在生成 PTX 前把整条调用替换成方言 op。可以把桩看成写给编译器的一张「请在这里插入一条 GPU 指令」的便签。
- **LLVM 内联汇编约束串**：形如 `"=f,=f,=f,=f,f,f,f,f,r,r,r,r,r,r"`，逗号分隔每个输出/输入寄存器。`=` 开头是输出，`f` 是浮点寄存器，`r` 是 32 位整型寄存器，`l` 是 64 位整型寄存器。模板里的 `$0`、`$1` … 按约束串顺序编号。本讲第 4.5 节会看到 mma 如何用它把 fragment 重新排成 PTX 要求的 `D, A, B, C` 顺序。

## 3. 本讲源码地图

| 文件 | 作用 | 本讲定位 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| [crates/cuda-device/src/wmma.rs](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/blob/29396b7f643b1d42eb4d80b7347ad27bb011525a/crates/cuda-device/src/wmma.rs) | 用户面设备 API：movmatrix / ldmatrix / 各 dtype 的 mma.sync 桩函数 | 第 1 层：设备层 API |
| [crates/dialect-nvvm/src/ops/wmma.rs](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/blob/29396b7f643b1d42eb4d80b7347ad27bb011525a/crates/dialect-nvvm/src/ops/wmma.rs) | `nvvm.mma_*` 方言 op 定义 + `Verify` + `register` | 第 2 层：dialect op |
| [crates/mir-importer/src/translator/terminator/intrinsics/wmma.rs](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/blob/29396b7f643b1d42eb4d80b7347ad27bb011525a/crates/mir-importer/src/translator/terminator/intrinsics/wmma.rs) | 把 mma 调用翻译成方言 op（拆 fragment 数组→建 op→装回数组） | 第 3 层：importer 翻译 |
| [crates/mir-importer/src/translator/terminator/mod.rs](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/blob/29396b7f643b1d42eb4d80b7347ad27bb011525a/crates/mir-importer/src/translator/terminator/mod.rs) | `try_dispatch_intrinsic` 大 `match`：按 FQDN 派发到上面的 emitter | 第 3 层：派发入口 |
| [crates/mir-lower/src/convert/intrinsics/wmma.rs](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/blob/29396b7f643b1d42eb4d80b7347ad27bb011525a/crates/mir-lower/src/convert/intrinsics/wmma.rs) | 把 `nvvm.mma_*` op 降级为 convergent 内联 PTX | 第 4 层：lowering |
| [crates/mir-lower/src/convert/interface_impls.rs](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/blob/29396b7f643b1d42eb4d80b7347ad27bb011525a/crates/mir-lower/src/convert/interface_impls.rs) | 为每个 op 实现 `MirToLlvmConversion`，派发到上面的 converter | 第 4 层：接口接线 |
| [crates/mir-lower/src/convert/intrinsics/common.rs](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/blob/29396b7f643b1d42eb4d80b7347ad27bb011525a/crates/mir-lower/src/convert/intrinsics/common.rs) | `inline_asm_convergent` 等公共 helper | 第 4 层：辅助 |
| [crates/llvm-export/src/export/state.rs](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/blob/29396b7f643b1d42eb4d80b7347ad27bb011525a/crates/llvm-export/src/export/state.rs) | `is_convergent_intrinsic`：仅对走 LLVM intrinsic 路径的指令需登记 | 第 5 层：导出（mma 走 inline asm，本步可跳过） |
| [cuda-oxide-book/compiler/adding-new-intrinsics.md](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/blob/29396b7f643b1d42eb4d80b7347ad27bb011525a/cuda-oxide-book/compiler/adding-new-intrinsics.md) | 官方「新增 intrinsic」手册，给出五阶段流水线与清单 | 全程参考 |

## 4. 核心概念与源码讲解

### 4.1 五阶段流水线总览

#### 4.1.1 概念说明

cuda-oxide 把「教编译器一条新的 GPU 指令」这件事做成了**机械流程**。每条 intrinsic 都沿同一条管道走，管道有五个工位，分别住在五个 crate 里。关键认知是：**五个工位各管一段，谁都不能替别人干活**。

- 设备层只管「给用户一个能调的 Rust 函数」和文档化它的安全契约。
- 方言层只管「在 IR 里有一个可校验的节点」。
- 翻译层只管「认出用户的调用，发射方言 op」。
- lowering 层只管「把方言 op 翻成 LLVM 能理解的形式」。
- 导出层只管「在文本 LLVM IR 上挂对属性」。

少任何一层，intrinsic 都会在某个阶段断掉：缺设备层用户没法调；缺方言层翻译层无处发射；缺翻译层调用落到普通函数调用；缺 lowering 层 op 永远停在 dialect-nvvm 进不了 LLVM IR；缺导出层（仅当走 LLVM intrinsic 路径且是 convergent 时）LLVM 可能把指令 hoist 出 `if` 块导致结果错误或死锁。

#### 4.1.2 核心流程

官方手册给出的五阶段流水线（注意它画出的是「翻译→方言」的依赖方向，与运行时数据流一致）：

```text
cuda-device          用户写：  mma_m16n8k16_f32_f16(c, a, b)
    │
    ▼
mir-importer         编译器看到调用，发射一个 dialect-nvvm op
    │
    ▼
dialect-nvvm         op 作为可校验的 IR 节点住在这里
    │
    ▼
mir-lower            把 dialect-nvvm op 转成 LLVM dialect op（内联 PTX）
    │
    ▼
llvm-export          导出文本 LLVM IR  →  llc 转成 PTX
```

在 lowering 工位，要在两条策略里二选一：

| 策略 | 何时用 | 例子 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| LLVM intrinsic 调用 | LLVM 已有内建 | `threadIdx_x`、warp shuffle、barrier |
| 内联 PTX 汇编 | 没有 LLVM intrinsic，或需要对 PTX 指令做精确控制 | `mma.sync`、`wgmma`、`tcgen05`、`mbarrier`、`trap` |

本讲的 mma 走的是**内联 PTX** 路径——PTX 里张量核指令语义太专门，LLVM 没有对应 intrinsic，必须直接发汇编。

#### 4.1.3 源码精读

五阶段图来自官方手册：

- [cuda-oxide-book/compiler/adding-new-intrinsics.md:L14-L32](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/blob/29396b7f643b1d42eb4d80b7347ad27bb011525a/cuda-oxide-book/compiler/adding-new-intrinsics.md#L14-L32) 给出 `cuda-device → mir-importer → dialect-nvvm → mir-lower → llvm-export` 的管道图。
- 同文件末尾的「Quick-Reference Checklist」[adding-new-intrinsics.md:L450-L473](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/blob/29396b7f643b1d42eb4d80b7347ad27bb011525a/cuda-oxide-book/compiler/adding-new-intrinsics.md#L450-L473) 列出每一步要动哪个文件——这是本讲的工程主线，后面 4.2–4.6 会逐条用真实 mma 源码落地它。

#### 4.1.4 代码实践

**实践目标**：在脑里建立「一次调用、六种形态」的整体感。

**操作步骤**：阅读手册的端到端对照表，它用 `threadIdx_x`（最简单）和 `shuffle_xor`（带参数）两条 intrinsic 展示同一次 Rust 调用在六种表示下的样子。

- [adding-new-intrinsics.md:L424-L447](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/blob/29396b7f643b1d42eb4d80b7347ad27bb011525a/cuda-oxide-book/compiler/adding-new-intrinsics.md#L424-L447)

**需要观察的现象**：注意 `Rust → MIR → Pliron MIR → Pliron LLVM → LLVM IR → PTX` 六种表示里，每一格都只比上一格「变了一点点」——这种小步、可校验、可独立测试的转换正是 cuda-oxide 流水线设计的核心动机。

**预期结果**：你能用自己的话回答「为什么中间要塞这么多表示，而不是直接从 Rust 生成 PTX」。

#### 4.1.5 小练习与答案

**练习 1**：把五阶段流水线的五个 crate 名按执行顺序写出来。

> **答案**：`cuda-device` → `mir-importer` → `dialect-nvvm`（作为 IR 节点的住所）→ `mir-lower` → `llvm-export`。注意「方言层」在数据流上是 importer 的产物、lowerer 的输入，所以它夹在两者之间。

**练习 2**：如果一个 intrinsic 走「LLVM intrinsic 调用」路径而不是「内联 PTX」路径，lowering 代码应该用哪个 helper？

> **答案**：用 `call_intrinsic`（见 u6-l3 与手册 Stage 4）；内联 PTX 才用 `inline_asm_convergent` / `inline_asm_sideeffect`。
